# Extracao: transcription_mp4_01-_Cardboard_Knight

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_01-_Cardboard_Knight.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

I'm eight years old and I'm digging a hole in my backyard underneath a pine tree. There's a bucket of coal leaning against the tree and I have been digging all afternoon and it is the summer and I'm hot and I'm tired, but I need to keep digging. I need to make this hole as deep as possible to make my plan work. I'm digging this hole because of another hole. The key feature in my mother's 1972 cherry red Chevy Chevette is the basketball size hole that's rusted out in the floor in front of the passenger seat and I love this hole. My brother and I take our Batman and Robin action figures and we tie rope around their wastes and we drop them through the hole like we're ice fishermen while she's driving down the highway and we let out the line until we can see Batman and Robin bouncing behind us and then we tie it off and we climb into the way back and we watch as Batman and Robin sort of ricochet off the pavement and each other and on one glorious day another car. So while we're back there one day watching Batman and Robin, I see that my sister Kelly who's two years younger than me, she's about five, she gets out of her seat and she starts heading towards the front of the car. She wants to see the hole I know and it's the first time she's tried to do this and she's tiny, she's a little, little girl and she should not be going near that rusted out hole and I know it and I'm waiting for my mother to say stop, like don't do that or even shove her back into the back seat and my mother's just driving and she's not saying a word and Kelly is climbing over the console and now she's in the seat and I'm waiting and waiting and finally I reach out and I grab her by the back of the collar and I reach and bring her into the seat and I say no and she says what in that cute little voice she has and I'm not sure what to say for a second and then I say you have to be seven to sit in the front seat near the hole because that seems sensible to me and Kelly agrees It's not the first time I've ever had to bridge the gap between parents and parenting for my parents. I grew up thinking that my parents were sort of like roommates who occasionally filled the refrigerator but didn't do very much else. I grew up without any rules. I had no bedtime, no one ever checked my homework and when the street lights went on and my friends had to go inside the house I got to stay out as long as I wanted and it sounds wonderful a great way to grow up except I was the eldest and when your parents don't do their job you feel like you have to do it for them and this was the 1970s. It was a dangerous time to be a child. There were no seat belts, there were no like life jackets being worn, there were no airbags or safety Tylenol caps that you couldn't unscrew like kids were riding in the back of pickup trucks and there was secondhand smoke everywhere. It was a time when you needed your parents to say no and my parents said nothing and so I had to be the voice of reason but I never stopped hoping that my parents wouldn't step up I kept thinking and hoping and praying that someday they would realize that they were parents and they would start doing their job and then everything changed the summer I went into third grade. We were staying with my aunt Diane while my parents were at school and aunt Diane had a cat named Prudence. It was the most evil cat that has ever lived on this earth. It was like Kujo but a cat. It was the Kaiser Soce of cats. I would come home and we would be clawed on our hands and our legs and I would show my mother and she would say you got to stay away from that cat. And then one day the cat cornered Kelly in the toy room and I couldn't get to Kelly quick enough and the cat clawed my sister's face three long gouges on her cheek and as angry as I was about it I thought to myself good now at least she will do something about the god damn cat but when we got in the car my mother saw my sister's face and she said I told you to stay away from that cat and I was pissed so I got home and I got a pair of scissors and I went into the basement and I started cutting up cardboard boxes and then I took twine and I started connecting the pieces with the twine and when I was all done I packed it into a bag and the next day I went to Diane's house with it and we went in the toy room and we closed the door and I took it out and started putting it on a suit of cardboard armor. My brother strapped it to my body and when it was fully on we opened the door and waited for prudence to come in and when she walked in Jeremy closed the door and left and I stood my ground for the first time against that god damn cat and every inch of my body that was not covered with cardboard was scratched and clawed and bitten but I did not give ground and after ten minutes I opened that door and that cat ran out of the room and it never bothered us again and I got in the car that night and I wanted to tell my mother what I had done. I wanted to tell her mostly because I wanted her to know that you don't have to let your kids get scratched and clawed that you can actually do something but I instantly realized it was a waste of time. My mother was never going to fight a war for me. She was never going to fight our battles, she was never going to put on armor and that was the day I gave up hope and so that's why I'm digging that hole underneath that tree because I read in a library book that diamonds are actually coal that have been buried for a really long time and I am eight years old and so I think that I can take that coal and I can bury it in the ground and when I'm 18 years old and we're really on our own and I have to take care of my brother and sister I'm going to come back to this tree and I'm going to dig up this hole and I'm going to have diamonds and that is what I am going to use to take care of my brother and sister and it doesn't happen. Science does not allow that to happen and it doesn't go well for us but that one day I'm looking at that hole that one day I felt like our future would be good. Thank you.
